fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Your primary international diversification engine. S&P 500 gives exposure to 500 of the world's largest companies (Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia etc.) — businesses that are simply not available on Indian exchanges. The quantitative case is hard to argue with: Sharpe ratio of 2.65 is the highest in the entire universe, meaning you get the most return per unit of risk — exactly what a Sharpe-maximiser wants. Beta vs Nifty 50 is just 0.07, meaning it moves almost completely independently of Indian markets — this dramatically reduces portfolio-level correlation and improves the overall risk-adjusted return of the combined portfolio. Yearly fee: 0.20% — near-zero cost. Tax: equity-taxed (≥65% equity). LTCG 12.5% on gains above ₹1.25L after 1 year.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential NASDAQ 100 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: A high-octane satellite to the S&P 500 core. NASDAQ 100 is concentrated in the world's most innovative technology companies — higher growth potential but also higher volatility (21% vs 16% for S&P 500). Sharpe of 2.14 is still exceptionally strong. Beta vs Indian market is 0.16 — still near-zero correlation with Nifty, adding further diversification. Combined with the S&P 500 allocation, your total international exposure hits 30%, which meaningfully decouples your wealth from India-specific risk events. Yearly fee: 0.20%. Tax: equity-taxed.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: India's mid-cap segment has historically delivered the best long-term returns among domestic equity categories. This index fund captures the Nifty Midcap 150 — the 150 companies just below the top 50 — at a yearly fee of just 0.20%, which is the same cost as an active fund's management overhead. 5-year CAGR of 18.7%, Sharpe of 1.00. Critically, this is the passive, low-cost way to get mid-cap exposure rather than paying 1.15% to an active manager. Fee comparison: over 18 years, the 0.95% fee saving vs active mid-cap funds compounds to roughly 18-20% more corpus for identical gross returns. Equity-taxed.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: This is the one active mid-cap fund that justifies its 1.15% fee — and here's the math: 3-year CAGR of 26.56% vs the index fund's 23.19% — an active outperformance (alpha) of +13.73% annualised, Sharpe of 1.12. After subtracting the 0.95% fee premium over the index, net alpha still stands at ~12.8% over 3 years. A 30-year fund house with ₹8,172 Cr AUM providing institutional-grade liquidity. It is held alongside the index fund deliberately — if the active fund's alpha degrades over time (which you should check annually), the index fund already provides the passive beta. Equity-taxed.

fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: Highest domestic 3-year CAGR in the small-cap category at 31.33%, with a 5-year CAGR of 23.71%. Small-cap is the highest expected-return domestic asset class over 18-year horizons — essential for hitting the ₹12 Cr target. The Sharpe of 0.59 looks lower because small-cap is inherently volatile, but the absolute return per unit of downside is superior over long periods. Beta of 0.92 vs Nifty shows controlled market sensitivity. This is SIP-only — small cap is NOT suitable for a lumpsum deployment due to valuation concentration risk. Equity-taxed. Critical warning: this fund can drop 40-50% in a severe bear market — you will not sell.

fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: The portfolio's volatility dampener and a second international diversification layer. PPFAS holds ~25-35% in overseas stocks (Google, Meta, Amazon etc.) directly in its portfolio, which is unique among Indian flexi-cap funds. The data tells the story: volatility of just 10.5% vs the category average of ~16%, and beta of 0.55 vs Nifty — meaning when Indian markets drop 10%, this fund typically drops only ~5.5%. Alpha of 11.6% shows genuine stock-picking skill. The ₹88,404 Cr AUM is the largest in the category — institutional stability. This fund is the shock absorber that prevents your portfolio Sharpe from collapsing during Indian market downturns. Equity-taxed.

fund.name: WhiteOak Capital ELSS Tax Saver Fund Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: Pure tax arbitrage play. As a 30% slab investor, ₹1.5L/year invested here saves ₹46,800 in income tax annually under Section 80C — that's an immediate 31.2% guaranteed return on the first ₹1.5L deployed, before the fund even does anything. Over 18 years, if you reinvest those annual tax savings of ₹46,800, at even 12% CAGR, they compound to an additional ~₹34L. WhiteOak was chosen over older ELSS funds: 3-year CAGR of 20.95%, lowest tracking error (6.18%) in the ELSS universe — meaning it's genuinely active, not a closet index fund in disguise. Low beta of 0.93 keeps drawdowns manageable. 3-year lock-in per SIP instalment. Equity-taxed post lock-in, with 80C benefit on entry.

fund.name: UTI Nifty 500 Value 50 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Tactical factor-tilt satellite position. This tracks the Nifty 500 Value 50 — 50 deep-value stocks from the broader Nifty 500, a factor (value) that has academically demonstrated long-run outperformance over cap-weighted indices. The 3-year CAGR of 31.14% and Sharpe of 1.48 at just 0.20% yearly fee is extraordinary. Small allocation only (part of the 'other' category) due to shorter track record (2y), kept as a diversifying factor bet. This is the quant investor's edge: factor exposure via low-cost passive — not active manager risk.

plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
**passive_instrument_fraction (0.62):** Looking at the equity sleeve only, the plan has four index funds and four active funds. By allocation weight: S&P 500 Index (20%) + NASDAQ 100 Index (10%) + Nifty Midcap 150 Index (20%) + UTI Nifty 500 Value 50 Index (0% allocation but ₹2,500 SIP — negligible) = ~50% passive equity. Active: Nippon India Mid Cap (15%) + Bandhan Small Cap (15%) + Parag Parikh Flexi Cap (10%) + WhiteOak ELSS (10%) = 50% active equity. The UTI Value 50 has 0% lumpsum allocation and a minor SIP, so the split is approximately 50/50. Slightly rounding up for the minor UTI SIP gives ~0.51, but the plan explicitly frames the index funds as the core and active as justified satellites, nudging the score to ~0.62 in spirit — actually, sticking to weights: ~50% passive out of 100% equity gives 0.50, but the UTI SIP at ₹2,500/month nudges it marginally above. Score: **0.52**, adjusting to 0.55 for the UTI tilt. Wait — re-evaluating strictly by allocation_pct: Index funds total = 20+10+20+0 = 50%; Active = 15+15+10+10 = 50%. Score: **0.50**. Rounding to 0.50 but keeping at 0.52 given the small UTI SIP. Final: **0.52**.

*(Self-correcting inline: final answer will reflect 0.52 for this dimension.)*

**turnover_score (0.72):** No explicit rebalancing guidelines or review checkpoints are provided (both fields are empty). The rationale mentions an annual check of active fund alpha ("which you should check annually"), suggesting annual review cadence at most. The long 18-year horizon framing and buy-and-hold language ("you will not sell") imply low turnover. This is close to buy-and-hold, but the annual alpha-check hint and the tactical framing of some positions (UTI Value 50 as a "tactical satellite") prevent a top score.

**cost_emphasis_score (0.75):** Cost minimisation is a prominently recurring theme. The plan explicitly quantifies the fee savings of index funds vs active (0.95% premium), projects the compounding impact over 18 years, and uses fee comparison as a primary justification for index fund selection. However, active funds are retained when alpha justifies the fee — so cost is a major but not singular criterion.

**research_vs_cost_score (0.55):** The plan blends both. For passive funds, it relies on broad market/factor exposure with no individual stock analysis. For active funds (Nippon Mid Cap, Bandhan Small Cap, PPFAS, WhiteOak ELSS), it references quantitative metrics (alpha, Sharpe, AUM, tracking error) but does not perform deep fundamental stock-level research. The active fund selection is based on quantitative screening of fund-level metrics rather than bottom-up stock analysis — this sits in the middle, slightly tilted passive.

**time_horizon_alignment_score (0.85):** The plan is explicitly designed for an 18-year wealth-building horizon, with strong language about not selling during bear markets ("you will not sell"), SIP discipline, and compounding. The empty review checkpoints reinforce a long-term, low-intervention approach. Minor deduction for the annual alpha-check and tactical satellite framing.

**portfolio_activeness_score (0.45):** The equity index funds (S&P 500, NASDAQ 100, Midcap 150, Value 50) are genuinely passive instruments — low cost, rules-based. However, the international index funds show anomalously high tracking error (21-25%) and alpha vs Nifty 50, which is an artefact of using Nifty 50 as the universal benchmark — these are USD-denominated indices and the comparison is not meaningful. Treating them as passive (which they are by category/rationale). The active funds — Nippon Mid Cap (beta 1.01, TE 8.59%, alpha 13.73%), Bandhan Small Cap (TE 11.72%, alpha 9.78%), PPFAS Flexi Cap (beta 0.55, TE 9.35%, alpha 11.63%), WhiteOak ELSS (TE 6.18%, alpha 8.21%) — show genuine active characteristics with meaningful tracking error and positive alpha. With 50% of the portfolio in genuinely active funds showing high TE and alpha, and 50% in passive instruments, the portfolio sits in the middle with a slight active tilt due to the quality of active management. Score: **0.45**.

pqs.reasoning:
**goal_alignment (0.72):**
The plan broadly aligns with Sheldon's aggressive profile, 18-year horizon, and ₹12 Cr retirement target. The heavy equity tilt (100% equity across all funds) is appropriate. International diversification (30%) matches the stated preference. ELSS addresses 80C savings. However, serious gaps exist: (1) There are no projected corpus calculations tying the SIP + lumpsum to the ₹12 Cr target — a glaring omission for an investor who demands quantitative rationale and wants the figure "calculated to the last rupee." (2) The plan ignores the research institute / sabbatical goal entirely — no separate bucket, no timeline, no sizing. (3) No Sharpe-maximisation framework is articulated at the portfolio level — individual fund Sharpes are cited but no portfolio-level Sharpe estimate is offered. (4) review_checkpoints, rebalancing_guidelines, projected_returns, and setup_phase are all empty, leaving the plan structurally incomplete.

**diversification (0.82):**
The plan covers multiple geographies (US via S&P 500 + NASDAQ 100, India via mid/small/flexi-cap + value factor), multiple market-cap segments (large-cap implicit in PPFAS and ELSS, mid-cap, small-cap), and multiple fund houses (Motilal Oswal, ICICI Pru, Nippon, Bandhan, PPFAS, WhiteOak, UTI — seven houses). Factor diversification is introduced via the value tilt fund. International allocation at 30% is meaningful. Weaknesses: (1) Zero fixed-income / debt allocation — for an 18-year horizon this may be acceptable at age 34 but there is no rebalancing glide-path or discussion of when to introduce bonds; (2) The UTI Value 50 fund is assigned 0% allocation but ₹2,500 SIP — internally inconsistent; (3) NASDAQ 100 and S&P 500 have substantial overlap (top NASDAQ names dominate the S&P 500), so the effective US tech concentration is higher than the 30% headline suggests.

**risk_return_appropriateness (0.75):**
An all-equity portfolio suits the aggressive appetite and 18-year horizon well. Small and mid-cap together represent 35% which is high-octane but defensible at age 34 with zero liabilities. The plan correctly flags that small-cap can drop 40-50% and instructs not to sell. PPFAS's low beta acts as a portfolio dampener. However: (1) The cited Sharpe and alpha figures for international index funds (alpha of 49 and 51 vs Nifty) are meaningless since the funds track USD-denominated indices — comparing against a Nifty benchmark produces spurious alpha and beta; this calls into question the plan's quantitative rigour. (2) The tracking error for the S&P 500 fund (21.39%) is extremely high for a passive fund and is not explained. (3) No downside scenario analysis (e.g., what happens to the corpus if markets fall 30% in year 15) is provided. (4) No SIP step-up rate is specified, leaving the ₹12 Cr target unvalidated.

**internal_consistency (0.55):**
Several contradictions and omissions undermine consistency: (1) The UTI Nifty 500 Value 50 fund has allocation_pct = 0.0 but monthly_sip_inr = ₹2,500 — the numbers are directly contradictory. (2) The plan mentions comparing active mid-cap fund alpha after "subtracting 0.95% fee premium" but the fee saving argument made for the index fund earlier uses the same 0.95% difference — yet the plan simultaneously holds both funds at a combined 35% mid-cap weight without resolving how this split will be managed or when the active fund would be dropped. (3) Key structural fields (setup_phase, review_checkpoints, rebalancing_guidelines, projected_returns, risks, sip_step_up) are all empty, making the plan incomplete. (4) The total SIP adds to ₹150,000 (30k+15k+30k+22.5k+22.5k+15k+12.5k+2.5k = 150k ✓) and lumpsum adds to ₹2.4M of the ₹4M corpus — the undeployed ₹1.6M is not explained. (5) The S&P 500 fund's beta vs Nifty of 0.07 and alpha of 49 are cited approvingly as evidence of diversification benefit, but these figures are artefacts of benchmark mismatch, not genuine low correlation — a contradiction with the plan's "data-driven" premise.

**tax_efficiency (0.82):**
This is the plan's strongest dimension. The rationale explicitly: (1) identifies the investor as a 30% slab taxpayer; (2) calculates the 80C saving (₹46,800/year) and compounds it over 18 years; (3) selects ELSS specifically to fill the 80C headroom; (4) confirms equity-tax treatment for all funds; (5) notes LTCG rates (12.5% above ₹1.25L after 1 year). All funds chosen are ≥65% equity, so none are exposed to slab-rate debt taxation — avoiding the key trap for a 30% investor. Minor deductions: (1) No mention of LTCG harvesting strategy (selling up to ₹1.25L gains annually tax-free); (2) No discussion of whether the ₹1.6M undeployed corpus should go into arbitrage funds (equity-taxed, near-debt risk) rather than a savings account while awaiting deployment; (3) The ELSS fund chosen (WhiteOak, ₹42 Cr AUM) has very low AUM raising liquidity concerns that are not addressed.
